g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 5: Grid-based Approach in Machine Learning. This powerful technique revolutionizes clustering by operating on grid cells instead of individual data points.
Traditional algorithms like K-Means struggle with large datasets. Processing each point individually becomes computationally expensive and does not scale well.
The fundamental idea is to quantize the data space into cells forming a grid structure, then perform clustering on these cells rather than individual data points.
g.text: Core Idea: Quantize Space into Grid

g.data-narration: A grid cell is a rectangular unit in the partitioned space. In 2D, it is defined by intervals on both x and y axes. Each cell captures all data points that fall within its boundaries.
g.text: Defined by intervals in each dimension

g.data-narration: Cell density is the count of data points falling into a cell. Cells exceeding a density threshold are labeled dense and form the foundation for cluster formation.
g.text: Density = Number of points in cell
Threshold tau: If density greater than or equal to tau then Dense cell

g.data-narration: Step 1: Create the grid by dividing the data space into a user-specified number of intervals per dimension, forming a multi-dimensional grid structure.
g.text: Divide data space into grid cells. For 2D: m by n grid. For n-dim: hyper-grid.
Grid size affects quality vs speed

g.data-narration: Step 2: Map each data point to its corresponding grid cell based on the point attribute values. This quantizes continuous data into discrete cells.
g.text: Each data point is mapped to the specific cell it falls into based on coordinate values.

g.data-narration: Step 3: Calculate the density of each cell by counting points. Step 4: Identify dense cells where density exceeds the predefined threshold.
g.text: Step 3 and 4: Density Calculation and Dense Cell Identification
Sparse cells become noise or outliers

g.data-narration: Step 5: Connect adjacent dense cells to form clusters. Two cells are neighbors if they share a face, edge, or vertex. The cluster is the union of connected dense cells.
g.text: Connect neighboring dense cells to form clusters. Neighbors share faces, edges, or vertices.

g.data-narration: Grid-based methods offer fast processing with O(n) complexity, handle noise by ignoring sparse cells, discover arbitrary cluster shapes, and support incremental updates.
STING (Statistical Information Grid) is a hierarchical grid algorithm. It creates multiple resolution levels, storing statistical data in each cell for efficient query processing.
g.text: STING creates hierarchical grid levels. Each cell stores statistical info: mean, std dev, min, max, distribution type.

g.data-narration: In summary: Grid-based approach partitions space into cells, operates on cells not points, achieves O(n) time complexity, and discovers arbitrary shaped clusters.
g.text: Partition space into finite cells - operate on cells, not individual data points
High speed and scalability with O(n) linear complexity

g.data-narration: Grid-based methods are used for spatial data clustering, customer segmentation, image processing, and form the basis for algorithms like STING and CLIQUE.
g.text: Spatial data mining - Customer segmentation - Image analysis
STING, CLIQUE - Subspace clustering - High-dimensional data
